ks1.objective: Dribble your ball away to hide while the seeker does toe taps, then dodge when they come looking.
successCriteria: Keeps the ball close while moving
Takes turns being Wally and the seeker

lks2.objective: Use close ball control and quick changes of direction to evade the seeker after the toe-tap count.
successCriteria: Uses small touches to stay agile
Scans for where the seeker is heading
Changes direction sharply to lose the seeker

uks2.objective: Apply specific surfaces of the foot and weaker-foot dribbling under tag pressure.
successCriteria: Uses inside / sole / weaker foot deliberately
Maintains top-speed dribbling when space is available

space.description: An area set up for the children to dribble around in — part of or the whole pitch, depending on numbers.

value.name: Storytelling
value.text: Introduce famous Where's Wally characters — Wilma and Wizard Whitebeard. Begin each round with a new exciting scene for the children to explore and hide in.

steps: Pair the children up. One in each pair is Wally; the other is the seeker.
The seeker begins by completing ten toe taps on the spot.
During this time, Wally must dribble their football away and hide within the area.
Once the toe taps are done, the seeker lifts their head, scans the area and tries to find and tag Wally.
When Wally is tagged, the pair swap roles and play again.
progressions: Rotate pairs regularly so children face different challenges.
Change the seeker's starting task from toe taps to inside touches, step-overs or keepy-ups.
Challenge Wally to dribble using their weaker foot only.
Challenge Wally to use specific surfaces of the foot, e.g. inside only.
Remove the seeker's football — instead of tagging, they must tackle and recover possession before swapping roles.
questions: What helps you stay hidden when there are lots of children in the area?
Which surface of the foot gives you the closest control?
How can you change direction without slowing down?

step.space: Smaller area = closer control + quicker direction changes; bigger area = top-speed dribbling
step.task: Set specific dribbling surfaces or weaker-foot only
step.equipment: Use brighter / different coloured bibs to make Wallys easier or harder to spot
step.people: Pair children of similar ability for first round, then mix

base.showmanship: Incorporate storytelling — introduce Wilma and Wizard Whitebeard. Begin each round with a new exciting scene for the children to explore and hide in.
